skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Playwright workflow for testing local web applications, inspecting rendered pages, capturing screenshots, and collecting browser console logs.
skill.summary: Test and debug local web apps with focused Playwright automation.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static high-risk hits in LICENSE.txt and SKILL.md are false positives from license prose and markdown text. The meaningful risk is a helper that runs user-provided server commands with shell=True and then executes an arbitrary follow-on command. No evidence found of malicious endpoints, credential access, or data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Shell Server Commands
medium_findings.description: The helper accepts --server values and passes them to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This is useful for local dev servers, but unsafe if command strings come from untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call explicitly enables shell=True for user-provided server strings. The behavior is intentional for local automation, so this is a real risk with a legitimate use case.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Follow-On Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The helper runs the remaining command arguments with subprocess.run after servers become ready. This can execute any local command under the user account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution path is direct and visible. It appears designed for running test scripts, but it still expands the skill capability to arbitrary local process execution.

medium_findings.title: Guidance Encourages Black-Box Script Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to run --help and avoid reading helper source unless customization is necessary. This can reduce review of a script that launches shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is explicit and relevant to execution safety. It is not a direct override of audit instructions, but it discourages source inspection before running bundled code.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network Examples Are Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to localhost or Apache license pages. I found no evidence of external telemetry, credential transfer, or suspicious network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited application URLs are localhost testing targets. The remaining URL findings are standard Apache license references, not runtime network behavior.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Writes Are Test Artifacts
low_findings.description: The examples write console logs and screenshots to fixed output paths. This is expected for browser testing but may expose page content in shared environments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The writes are clearly screenshots or logs generated by local tests. They are not hidden persistence, but they can contain rendered page data.

low_findings.title: License Text Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts occur in Apache License prose and markdown metadata, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or RC4 use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are legal text or descriptive metadata. There is no cryptographic API call or hash implementation at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied Shell Server Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper accepts --server values and passes them to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This is useful for local dev servers, but unsafe if command strings come from untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call explicitly enables shell=True for user-provided server strings. The behavior is intentional for local automation, so this is a real risk with a legitimate use case.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Follow-On Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper runs the remaining command arguments with subprocess.run after servers become ready. This can execute any local command under the user account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution path is direct and visible. It appears designed for running test scripts, but it still expands the skill capability to arbitrary local process execution.

content.user_title: Test Local Web Apps with Playwright
content.value_statement: Local web app testing often needs repeatable browser actions and clear debugging artifacts. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Playwright workflows for rendered pages.
actual_capabilities: Runs Playwright scripts against local web applications.
Starts one or more local development servers before automation.
Waits for configured localhost ports before running tests.
Captures screenshots for rendered page inspection.
Collects browser console messages during automation.
Discovers buttons, links, inputs, and visible element text.
limitations: Requires Playwright and browser dependencies in the local environment.
The server helper executes local commands supplied by the user.
Examples focus on localhost and static HTML workflows.
It does not provide a full test runner or assertion framework.

use_cases.title: Debug a Local Frontend
use_cases.description: Inspect rendered state, capture screenshots, and collect console messages after a local app loads.

use_cases.title: Verify UI Flows Before Release
use_cases.description: Run focused browser automation against a local dev server before merging a change.

use_cases.title: Explore Unknown Page Structure
use_cases.description: Discover visible buttons, links, and form fields before writing selectors for automation.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Local App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check of a running frontend.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill to open my local app at http://localhost:5173, wait for network idle, and report visible UI issues.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Console Logs
prompt_templates.scenario: Debug browser errors during page load.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to visit my local app, capture browser console messages, and summarize errors, warnings, and likely causes.

prompt_templates.title: Test a User Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Validate an interactive path with screenshots.
prompt_templates.prompt: Start my dev server, run a Playwright flow for sign-in and dashboard navigation, and capture screenshots at key states.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multiple Servers
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with frontend and backend services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the server helper for my backend and frontend commands, wait for both ports, then run a Playwright regression check.

output_examples.input: Check whether my local dashboard renders correctly.
output_examples.output: A short test report with page load status, visible dashboard sections, console warnings, and screenshot file locations.

output_examples.input: Find usable selectors for buttons and form fields.
output: List of visible buttons and labels.
List of input names, identifiers, and field types.

output_examples.input: Run a checkout flow on a local dev server.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step result summary covering actions completed, blocked selectors, console errors, and final page state.

best_practices: Review server commands before execution, especially commands copied from untrusted sources.
Wait for network idle before inspecting dynamic pages or selecting elements.
Store screenshots and logs in predictable output locations for later review.
anti_patterns: Do not run unknown server commands through the helper without review.
Do not inspect dynamic DOM state before the app finishes loading.
Do not rely only on brittle CSS selectors when roles or text are available.

faq.question: Does this skill test deployed websites?
faq.answer: It is written for local apps and static files. It can navigate URLs, but the examples focus on localhost.

faq.question: Does it start development servers?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper can start one or more local server commands and wait for their ports.

faq.question: Is the helper safe to run?
faq.answer: It is safe only when server commands are trusted. It uses local process execution and shell support.

faq.question: What artifacts can it produce?
faq.answer: It can produce screenshots, console log files, and written summaries of discovered page elements.

faq.question: Does it include assertions?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and examples, not a complete assertion framework.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
